speaker: interviewer
text: Hoffman. Fred Efraim Hoffman. Today is November the 5th, 1995. And we are conducting an interview for the Shoah Foundation. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Fred, we've jumped forward a little bit in your story. And what I would like to do is to give you an opportunity to go back a little bit to life before the war and kind of leading up to the war. What are some of the things that perhaps you've left out so far that you would like to share about your family life? About your neighborhood, about your parents? Some of those kinds of things.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I left out a few things which I'm going to come back to now. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] say a few things about the life at home. It was-- I'm going to talk about my mother a little bit. She had a shaytl because the Jewish religion calls when a girl gets married, she has to shave off her hair and wear a shaytl. So the Jewish women used to get together and cut each other's hair all the time when they were getting too long. And they were all wearing the shaytls. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Jewish girls were not bar mitzvah. Only the boys were bar mitzvah. And the bar mitzvah was going on that we had to study in the Cheder the Haftarah. And the father was when the time came, the mother baked a cake. The father bought a bottle of schnapps. Took it to the shul after I had the Haftarah. When I got bar mitzvah. All the boys when they got bar mitzvah. That was the cost and the style of the women there. We had a kiddish in the shul and that was it. We didn't make no big parties. The girls were never bar mitzvah in that time. We used to go to shul. The women were separated from the men. They had a separate place and a little window to look through. And that was the kind of religious life we were living there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were the women permitted to be a part of the service in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or to study the Torah?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Well, they didn't study the Torah. They were just listening to the men, what the men were saying and praying. And we didn't have any sermons either. We were just davening and then that was over. We went home. Unless somebody had a yahrzeit and bring some kiddish to the shul, everything was done in the synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you remember your mother and your sisters ever questioning that and saying that they wanted to be more a part of religious life?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. They didn't. There was no questions about it because it was just like that going on all the time. Also we didn't have any radios. We didn't have any televisions in that time. As a matter of fact, the town didn't have any electricity. We had to use those naphtha lamps. And when my father was working late at night, he had a lamp with a big bulb on it there. And that's-- he was working at night time like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you heat your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: We heat the home-- we had a stove and we had to put woods all night long. And my mother had to bake bread twice a week because we used a lot of cornbread. And cornbread dries up too fast. And you have to bake it twice a week. Also on Shabbos we had challah. We always had meat. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Shabbos wasn't entirely observed as a more like a little holiday. Like it was a different day on Shabbos. And we had meat on Shabbos or chicken and [NON-ENGLISH], as we call it. They used to bake it over night with beans. And then they had a kugel. It was feeling like we had a Jewish feeling in the house. It was a-- a Shabbos was a Shabbos. Nobody was working. And they would tell us stories. And studying the Chumash. And whatever it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did other families come to your home during the Shabbos?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we used to visit a lot. We used to go to our friends, and other Jewish people, and just keep in touch all the time. One time we went to them, the other Shabbos they came to us. And we have to know our Chumash very well. Because all the people used to call you, and ask you to read it for them, and to translate it to see if you know what you are reading about. And you better knew it otherwise you were ashamed. You know, you would kind of get red in the face and be very, very bad about it. So usually we knew those things.

speaker: interviewer
text: And for it being that you were a community of maybe 30 Jewish families, did your synagogue have a regular rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We didn't have a regular rabbi.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did your rabbi come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: We just had a rabbi come in from other towns, from the bigger cities. And that rabbi used to teach us also like the Cheder. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he didn't get paid. He was only working for food. Like Yeshiva [INAUDIBLE]. He used to come in. And he used to go from one Jewish house one week, and another Jewish house one week. All the children were getting together and learning their religion. The davening, the writing, the reading, and all those things. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] About communication, we didn't have anything. There was no radios. It was a very, very poor life over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how was it that you received news of what was going on in the world?

speaker: interviewee
text: News was received is one guy was assigned [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and he had a drum. And he used to beat the drum. And go every few streets and beat the drum. And all the people from the house went out. And then they announced the bulletin-- what they wanted to tell us. That was the communication we had.

speaker: interviewer
text: And would he be in a town square or would he walk around from one block--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was just walking from a few blocks to a few blocks. It was not a square. So half the whole town would know what was going on. If there's some news came into town and they wanted the people to know about it. That was the communication what was going on like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now I understand there was a story about when you were much younger and you almost died and someone saved you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a little cat in the house. First let me tell you this. We didn't even water in the house. We had a well outside by the house. And for the whole neighbors, we were taking the water and bringing it into the house. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The well was pretty deep. But it was also fenced around with rocks. Mixed also it was-- you could go down and up in it. But no children. And we had a wheel. And we had to turn the water up with a bucket, and then put it in your bucket, and take it in the house. You don't know. You never knew about this. We had a little cat in the house. And also on that well, it was a little cover outside. It was wintertime. And I played with the cat. And the cat ran out of the house, and I ran after the cat. The cat jumps on that little cover and jumps over. And I go the same way. I put my foot on that cover, and I want to jump over. And the cover turned, and I fell in the well. It was on a Sunday morning. The neighbor from across the street saw this whole thing. He was getting ready to go to church. He was combing his hair in the mirror, and he saw me fallen in the well. He runs out from the house, and runs to the well, and his wife runs after him. She doesn't know what happened to him. She thought he got crazy and wants to commit suicide.

speaker: interviewer
text: That guy comes down in that well. And I was already drowned because I couldn't do nothing-- I was four, five years old. And I was just drinking the water until I was dead. So the wife starts hollering at the man, what happened to you? What happened to you? He says, come on, grab the kid. He drowned.

speaker: interviewee
text: He jumped into the well. He pulled me up, and he turned me upside down, and shaked the water out of me. And I start breathing again and I got alive. And that was something-- I don't know. It was a miracle that I got alive again in that time. The neighbors were pretty nice to us. We were pretty friendly with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it sounds like your neighborhood was a mixture of Jews and non-Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Right. We were all spread out. We were not in one place. One Jew lived in here, one lived over there. We were all spread out. you were treated and you've got along very well-- Right. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very well, it sounds like. Now let's jump ahead a little bit. And we talked about the occupation in '39 by the Hungarians.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you talked some about what took place as a result of that. What was some of the progression between '39 and say, '40, '41, '42? As time went on in your town what changed?

speaker: interviewee
text: It didn't change anything much. We were just going on. Life was going on like that. We know that we are. Also the schools they're changing. Because after the '39 when the Czech moved back, when they got pushed out of Hungary, we had to start out learning a little Hungarian. We had to start now learning different languages. And we never finished any one of those languages. I could hardly speak any Hungarian, or any Ukrainian, or Czechoslovakian. Very few words. I mean, I understand more than I can say because it's so many years ago. And I was such a child in that time. And life was just going on. And we were just waiting to see what's going to happen. There was not too many changes being made in the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other occupations besides the Hungarians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, no. There wasn't. There was only the Hungarians. The Hungarians said to the Czech, this is our land and we want it back. And the Czechs, without any fight, they had to go back fast. They gave them a certain time to move out of that area. And they just left us. And then we were on the Hungarian regime.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any awareness through the-- what I would call town crier. The man with the drum who would come around and report the news. Was there any news of what was happening with the Germans, and the Nazis, and Hitler back during that period of your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was very quiet. It was like a surprise when the Germans came in. We didn't know that the Germans are going to come in. They just came in overnight. And all of a sudden, we found out that we are under the Germans now. That we are occupied under the Germans. And the Hungarians were very collaborating with the Germans. They were working together. And very pro-German.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was in '44?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the date the Germans came and occupied approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was about around end of April--

speaker: interviewee
text: 1944. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And prior to that, you talked about the changes where there was a curfew, when you couldn't move around very well, and things like that. What other sort of restrictions were placed on you as time went on before the occupation by the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they just-- they were planning of just-- it's really-- I can't remember anything what was going on in that time. It's hard to remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember there being a lot of food or was food scarce at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't because we were just living the way we lived before. We cooked at home. We had enough food to survive, to live long. And food was-- it wasn't meat everyday, but we were cooking everyday. It wasn't scary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father able to continue working in his shoe maker business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that continued as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: That continued until they took us out of the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now let's go to that time. And we had jumped ahead a little bit to talking about when you were taken from your home and you were taken to the first ghetto. When you arrived at that ghetto-- and the name again was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mateszalka.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: If was in Hungary. When we arrived in that ghetto, we seen a lot of people there already. It was a very big ghetto. Because they were taken all from all the bigger cities, and the smaller villages, and they were just putting them all in the same place. What will happen after, we didn't know in that time yet. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was a very hard trip to get to the ghetto, as I told you. It was-- the trains were slow, and it took about three to four days to arrive there. We came there. We didn't know what was going to be. They assigned us to a certain building. To a certain barrack. All the families were together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they tell you why you were going, Fred?

speaker: interviewee
text: They told us that we are going to go to work to Germany because they need the men for the war. And they have a lot of work to be done inland. And we will be working in Germany, and we will be well taken care of. We will be all OK. They just need us to work in Germany. That's what they told us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why you? What were you told about why--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry, but I have to do one adjustment.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] And what were you told about why you were selected to go? Why the people, your family, and other people that were on that transport? Why were you selected?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well they wanted the Jewish people only. That's what they wanted. They didn't touch any of the Christians or any other ones. They were just for the Jews to gather it off and to take them out of the villages. And that was the goal to do. I think that is the order from Hitler or somebody. Just to take all the Jewish people out there. There was no questioning. If you have a question, nobody could answer it for you because they were just for getting rid of the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anyone resisting at that time? Were there resistors and people saying no, I won't go? And if so, what happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they said, if there will be any resistance, you will be shot right away. You will have to obey the order, otherwise you will be shot and killed right away. So everybody was afraid to do anything. While we were already in their hands, you couldn't do anything about it. We were already victims of it. And we had to follow whatever they told us to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when we were at Mateszalka and they cleaned up all those cities, when they-- not cleaned up. When they took all the Jews out of them. When they took all the Jews out of the villages. When there was nobody left around that area. We were there about, I would say, a week and a half to two weeks. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then they start taking us over to the trains again. Were you still with your family when that happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We were the whole family together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Except for--

speaker: interviewee
text: Me, my mother, my father, my two sisters. My brothers-- we didn't know nothing about where they were.

speaker: interviewer
text: Still. Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were in the Hungarian army somewhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were put in the trains again after two weeks in the ghetto. As I said, when they cleared out all the Jews from the places. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They pushed us in about a hundred, if more, if not more, into a wagon-- cattle wagon again. And we arrived to Auschwitz. It took about four days also.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we get to Auschwitz, let me ask one more question about Mateszalka.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were there for the few weeks that you were there, what kinds of things did you do? What what was it like there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't do anything. We were just sitting down. We would make a little fire on the outside, on the ground, on wherever it was. And tried to cook a little food, which they allowed us to do that. So we eat. They gave us no food at all. We had to live on the food that we brought along from the house, from the home.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when we left the house, mostly what we took along was food, bread, and things like that. And little clothes, too. It was very little they allowed you to take with you. Did they give you water? Were there sanitation facilities?

speaker: interviewee
text: They gave us some water. Water was OK. We could find some water to drink. And we were cooking outside. Making some little [INAUDIBLE] there. Anything you could do just to have a little bit to eat. And every so often, apparently, they gave us a little food, too, sometime. There was thousands and thousands of people in that ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was in charge? Who was giving the orders at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans took over in that time. And we were in their hand. And they gave all the orders what to do, and when to move, and when to go, and all kind of things. They were in charge of us at that time. We were in their hands.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you remember about them? What were some of your feelings that you had at that time? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we know that we were going into Hell. We knew. We had feelings that we don't know what is going to happen. We might all die out and never come back again. We already had a feeling that this is end of all of us. That we will never survive anymore. Because the way they were treating us, and they were screaming, and hollering, and beating, and punishing us, and giving us nothing to eat. We had a feeling that this is it. Who knows what's going to be with us. But how we will die, how we will do, and what it going to happen later on is time to tell.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your faith, your religion? Your family was orthodox. Your father--

speaker: interviewee
text: We prayed a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you continue to pray?

speaker: interviewee
text: We prayed a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Even the ghetto. We gathered groups of little groups together. And we have to hide so they didn't see us. Because they did not allow any groups to be together at all. We were being watched from big towers through the guards. And they said, no groups together. But if you are three, four, five, a few people together, they let us do it. When we got together, we prayed every so often, and that's all we could do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember trying to get a Minyan together? Was that an important thing?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Minyan was important. But if you didn't have a Minyan, whatever how many people there it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: It didn't matter.

speaker: interviewee
text: It didn't matter. We just prayed together. We said a few words. And we kept the religion. We kept it in our heart. And we did not want to give up the religion.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Arriving to Auschwitz. I'm going to start here now.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: We finally arrive to Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We went out of the wagons of those trains. Freight trains, cattle trains, whatever you call it. They told us to move fast. They were screaming and hollering. And calling us, [GERMAN]. That means, get out fast. Move. [GERMAN]! Dogs! All kind of names. Whatever they could do us. We had to move fast. Get out of the wagons. And we went out of the wagons. We stayed in a line. And we start moving slowly to the front. There was SS people. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] All the families were huddling together. God forbid the children should be separated. We were holding on like this one here. All the families together and just marching forward in that line. When we came in front of that line, there were SS. One SS, a big one. I don't know what his name is. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He was making recht, links, recht, links. He was separating the men and the women. That was the first thing. The children went with the mothers. The fathers-- I mean the boys, the men-- went on the other side. Then we were continue going in the line. That's the last time I see of my sisters and my mother. From that time, I don't know what happened to them. I know what happened, but I'll tell you later. We start marching forward. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They took the old people. Links, left. The ones they could see that they can work, or be able to work, young boys, younger men, they moved them to one part. To one side. Other ones-- all the people what they were pretty old-- to the other side. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When we were all finished on that line, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we arrived to a place. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Big buildings. And that was in Auschwitz. We had to go on in that building. I was holding on to my father. [PAUSES FOR 21 SECONDS] We went in that building. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We had to take our clothes off. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had to go through a shower. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And on the other side of the building, there were lines of barbers, whatever they are. Shaved our head off, our hair. Shaved off, we all look alike. We took the shower. We went there. They shaved our hair. They was set up tables with striped clothes. We left all our clothes, all our shoes, everything there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We had to go to those tables and pick shirt, pants, a little hat. A cap, not a hat. And a little long-striped coat. And there was all the tables set up for those wooden shoes. Everybody took one of those, a pair of shoes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] From there, we went into the barracks. We were assigned to big bunks or something like. Big buildings. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] We were [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] staying there. Seeing people. Whatever before us there, just sitting around on the ground. On the floors. They were speaking different languages we could hardly understand. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they said to us that it's a very scary place. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Next morning, we were sleeping there. We had those bunk beds three floors up. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Next morning, I got up. I go outside. Then what's going on on the ground. We could walk around there in that area there. You could see the crematorium with the smoke coming out. It was maybe about two blocks away from us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we said, what is that smoke? They told us, this is where they burn the women, the people, the children, the old people. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] How do they do it? They said that they told them that are going to take showers. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The mothers with the children. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Instead of letting out water, they'll have gas. They gassed them. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You could smell the air from burnt bodies. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: We could see those little [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] wagons pushed by the people what they are in the camp. Dead bodies. Piles. Pushing to the oven, to the crematorium there. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Also they told us that we might all wind up being there. But who knows what is going to be.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was telling you that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The people what they were already in the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they didn't know that they will take us and ship us to other camps, to working camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: We thought first they would kill-- burn and gas the children. The mothers what they don't-- there was a lot of mothers that they ask if they want to leave the child go on to die, to the death, and the mother would go to the working camp. But no mother wanted to give up the children so they would just push them in there. The mothers with the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fred, what were-- you had described what was in the air and what it looked like. But what were some of the sounds you remembered hearing? Do you remember anything about that from your first impression?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were scared. Our minds were starting to get very-- we start to lose our minds already. We had a lot of fear in our heart.

speaker: interviewer
text: We thought, this is it. What is going to be? Why did they do it? What's happening? What did we do wrong to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: All kind of ideas came in the mind. Try to run away from there-- you couldn't. Because it was all electric wires around the fence. You couldn't go nowhere. You touch a wire, you will get an electrocute.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we didn't even know that we are going to be sent in to another camp. Did you hear voices on loudspeakers or were there a lot of orders being shouted out even that first day that you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We heard voices all day long. Screaming, and yelling, and hollers, and beating, and all in German language. There was always screaming going on. A lot of people were crying. So they-- it was unbelievable what was going on in that Auschwitz there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the lager that you were put in? Which barracks you were in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it's was called Birkenau. It was a gypsy camp there. There was a lot of gypsies there. And we were mixed in with them together. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They gave us a number.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your number?

speaker: interviewee
text: 57,866. I was not-- oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was not tattooed. I was not put a number on my arm and neither was my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
